feedback.text: I have some points i think should be addressed in the "digital fairness act" 1. I think that it incredibly unfair that websites/companies can use the word word "buy" or any other word that gives the idea of ownership, if said thing can be revoked in any way. So I think it should be words like "rent" if it is not a permanent access or change of ownership. But this is not only a digital problem, this also happens with cars. Some cars can just be turned off if the car company feels like it. "Because its in the EULA" 2. Speaking of EULAs. Companies should not be able to change an EULA after I've paid for something. Because that sure sounds like changing the terms of the sale. This happened to me with a video game i really liked "Risk of Rain 2", where i suddenly had to accept a new EULA to continue playing a game i bought 5-6 years ago. So all of a sudden i cant play something I've bought because. But if they really want to change the EULA, i think i should have the full right to a full refund. Since that is like walking into my house and saying, "if you want to keep this lamp, you need to sign this new contract, that's says "I can take this lamp from you at any point in the future", because i said so" I guess it is fair if they change the EULA on a subscription that has run out. But that then it should not automatically sign me up to the new one. Because that's not what i agreed on. And that's not fair. 3. In reference to "yes" "no" buttons, i think it all Yes/No buttons should be - the same size. - use same font. - have the same colour. - have the same transparency When used in correlation to - money/ buying, subscriptions. - private data/cookies settings - adds Because 90% of websites has a big blue or green button for "accept all cookies" and a small grey almost hidden button, that should be a reject cookies, but that's not what it is most of the times. That's a button goes to another menu where sometimes of the time is a no to all cookies, that's still less visible that the still BIG GREEN Yes button. Other times they have a "recommended cookies" button, that's also just also "accept all cookies". Other times you have to click a "reject all cookies" button, that does not do that, unless you also click the still not easy to find "save cookie preferences" button. See its confusing just to try and explain the ways that websites try to trick you into accepting cookies. And that's really unfair. Hope this gets taken up.
